speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven-- I'm interviewing Philip Katz. Philip, you were just describing your arrival in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewheres in the dark, we were driven to the side of a huge building, where we stood in line in the dark, and the line was moving forward towards one of these men in these stripes, who-- who was sitting there. And pretty soon, when I realized what he was doing, I saw that he was shaving the underarm and the pubic hairs of the people, who by then, were told to drop all their clothes in place, so to say, where they were. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] fate-- was a really a fortuitous or serendipitous circumstance, I looked at this young man, and he looked at me. And we recognized each other. We had studied together in Switzerland. And he then told me to stay in place, and let everybody come ahead of me. And as he was performing his duties, he talked to me. By the time it became finally my turn, there was nothing that could be known about Auschwitz that I didn't know. He had already been for four years in the concentration camps. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall this person's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. One of the things that he had told me was that the very important thing was to get out of Auschwitz. And the only way to get out was to be needed. And the best way to be needed was to be useful to what was considered the war industry. And, you're going to have to stop this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's try to ignore his voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And so in turn, I had converted everybody into a mechanic, telling them that when and if the time came where they would ask whatever trade or profession was, they should be mechanics. And eventually, all 1,200 of us who finally were mustered out and brought into the barrack, we all knew that we had to be mechanics. And finally, one day, there was an officer of the Organisation Todt-- which was the heavy construction organization of the German state corporation-- came in. And we were taken out one by one from the barrack. And he stood there with a proportional caliper in his hand, and I was asked to translate. And as each man approached, he would ask me to ask him what his profession was. And I would try telling-- this man wants to know whether you are a mechanic. And I turned back to him, and say, he says he's a mechanic. And for the sake of variety, I would say he's a bicycle mechanic, a motorcycle mechanic, or a locksmith, or a this, or a that. And so all 1,200 of us became mechanics. It's-- this is another aspect of this simplicity and almost primitivity, at least, of the whole procedure, that I could convert 1,200 people into mechanics. And you would have thought that the Germans in Hungary selected 1,200 mechanics from the whole country and brought them together and brought them to Auschwitz. Anyway, because we were mechanics, we were apparently, as it turned out to be later, hired out to the Air Force. And we left Auschwitz. There's a debate between myself and my sister whether it was two weeks after we arrived or three weeks after we arrived. And I was taken to France, to a place which I later discovered was Longwy. And the camp was outside this town called [FRENCH], which was a mining area. And we were to have brought a airplane fuselage factory into the mines. And then build the airplane fuselages. And this was the model, so to say, for the three other work camps that I was at, except for Dachau. It really was four camps. And except for Allach, it was all on the ground in mines. Originally in France, it was in ore mines, and Heilbronn and [INAUDIBLE] it was in salt mines. But I was-- as the airlines moved westward, or eastward for them, we were-- kept being evacuated. And I went from [INAUDIBLE] to either Heilbronn or Kochendorf or vice versa. I don't know exactly which came first. And then from there, I went to Dachau. And from Dachau, I went to Allach. And in Allach, we were working at the BMW factory, still supposedly building-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building airplane fuselages. I think I can confidently say that we, in the one year that I was shuttled from one camp to another, I-- I don't think that one fully completed piece of airplane fuselage ever left our factory. May have been half fabricated, but in most cases, obviously, we didn't know what the heck we were doing. And a lot of time was spent with [INAUDIBLE] it on our part or on the part of whatever forced laborers who were in the area, and who were enrolled in the same enterprise. And then from Allach, we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put in-- actually, at times, I had worked in other places, like in the airport at Munich, and the railroads at Munich that were bombed out by the Allies. And most of the times, I was called on to be a translator. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were sent out from Allach, we were shuttled, I would say, not more than about 30 kilometers. It took about a week because the rails were bombed out. And probably, there was no coal for the railroad. And maybe there wasn't even an engineer to drive the railroad. It-- we just kept moving, literally a kilometer or two, and then we would halt, and so this 30 kilometers to Tutzing-- T-U-N-I-Z, or zed, I-N-G. We were on Lake Starnberg, a rather famous resort area. And we finally halted at this railroad station. And we must have been there for two days or so. The highway, apparently, was running parallel to the railroad, maybe a few hundred yards away from the rails. And one fine day, we saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after we heard for the whole day the bombardment and the cannons, we saw, on the one hand, the Germans rushing helter skelter away from there, and these beige tanks with four or five pointed star, white star, coming towards us on the road. And then one of these tanks sort of peeled off, drove through the grass to the railroad, and some soldiers jumped out. We didn't know what kind of soldiers they were, until sort of it became clear that they were Americans. And we were told we were liberated. And prior to that, I had made one of my many attempts to escape, and I was shot in the heel. And I was kind of bloodied. And some friends of mine who were some fellow prisoners sort of picked me up. And they took me to a villa that was right next to the railroad station, and deposited me there with two German women, obviously a mother and daughter. Who, of course, made their usual disclaimer that they didn't know anything about it, and were very offended that they were being saddled with this unexpected guest. And subsequently a little while later, a day later-- again, the time is one mass-- I was taken to what used to be an SS hospital and sanatorium in Tutzing, in the city itself, or in the village itself. Well, by that time, the wounded SS or the recovering SS soldiers were sort of pushed out from the facilities-- well, the better part of the facilities. And the bullet was removed by an SS officer. It wasn't a deep wound. It was very superficial. Under the very close supervision of a couple of fellow prisoners, or former prisoners, who were standing near the operating table, ready to kill the SS doctor if something happened to me. And then I was there for a couple of days, dressed in the pajamas that these friends rustled up in the home of those two women. And when I felt strong enough, I picked myself up and walked in those pajamas towards Feldafing, which wasn't too far from there. Which was, by that time, known as being what eventually, we called it a DP camp, where Jews began to be collected by the American soldiers. And there, I became a member of the committee of seven that organized the DP camp. And and for all intents and purposes, ran it for the American military government. And worked in the American military government office as part of my duties, as a member of the committee. And of course, I volunteered my services to the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we go back for a few moments to Auschwitz, and the day that you arrived there? You heard from someone who you had once studied with.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Switzerland-- that your life and the lives of your friends and fellow inmates were in great peril.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. It was more than that. I mean, the man had four years of concentration camp existence by then. And he was thoroughly worsted in the concentration camp life and in the nature of the Holocaust. And he left absolutely nothing to the imagination.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall your thoughts at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I recall my thoughts? What do you think somebody thinks when he's told that? Look up at the smoke, and see there goes your mother. You have no thoughts. You are devastated. And you are-- you are drained of all normal mental processes. Your mind doesn't function. And the mind is accustomed to function. It's-- your mind becomes a black hole. You-- you can't disbelieve, and you can't believe. You can't fail to accept, but you can't realize it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Again, it was also part of the whole process of dehumanization. Perhaps this not planned by the Germans, because this was, as I say, a very unusual and fortuitous event. But the fact that people could, even if only as a rumor, hear, or even if they did not believe the odor or the sight of the smoke, even if it was only a doubt that was gnawing away at your mind, it completely devastated your mental processes. You were not an ordinary human being on the planet Earth. You were mentally, certainly, a zombie on a different planet, maybe a little green man on Mars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kept me going, me, you know, and people like myself, in the literature on the Holocaust, they say-- they are very clear on this. People who are aware and politically conscious, had a better chance of mental survival than those who were not. Intellectuals and intelligentsia may have had a diminished physical capacity or facility for survival than people, for instance, who were accustomed to heavy labor all through their life, or who were physically in better condition than the average intellectual would be. But on the other hand, the intellectual who has had political consciousness, had a better chance of mentally surviving it. And in a way, become more useful to his fellow prisoners because of that. And there are ample examples and proofs of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little about your life in Auschwitz for the few weeks that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my life was that of the millions who went through Auschwitz. We would-- we were housed in a wooden barrack, were sleeping on wooden pallets with maybe 12. And eventually, as it became more crowded-- and this would happen in every other camp. The physical setup was the same. They would pile in more than 12 or 15, where you literally couldn't move. And if you did move, the whole 15 had to move and readjust their position. At one end of the hall, there was a room where usually what they called the blockalteste, the elder of the block and the kapo would be housed, and where there was some administrative procedure maintained. And at the other end, there was a huge kettle or vat, like a metal vat which was-- played the role of a toilet. All night long, people would be marching to void and, well, which added a pretty pungent odor to-- to the barrack. In the winter-- and I wasn't there in the winter-- but in other camps, they would have one little tin oven, which sometimes was fired up to provide some heat. But basically, you know, the heat was provided by the-- however exhausting, however exhausted, the living organism of the human beings who were pressed in. And I mean, in [INAUDIBLE] for instance, I remembered that after-- after-- shortly after we finished building the camp, which is what we had to do first before we even went down to the mines-- they had-- they had halved the camp, after we barely moved in, and put women into the other half. So if we were 10 to our pallet, then we were 20 after that. So that there would be room for the-- for the women, who were also probably put 20 in a pallet. And which also had the kind of-- and I'm sure unintended result of seeing women. And seeing some of our women led a great many people to believe that-- oh, you know, the-- our women are alive. Not even many people realized that if they saw only young and more or less healthy-looking women that that meant that the mothers and the children were gone. Quite the contrary, that gave them this misleading hope. And again, it's that primitive psychology, that elementary psychology, you know, that played on the emotions and the beliefs of people. I remember so clearly when the women arrived that-- how everybody was absolutely convinced that our women were alive. And anyway, you know, the camp-- the camp life was obviously universally the same, with variations dependent on the camp commandant, or on the location, or on the climate. But basically, it was the same system. So you know, and I was in no way different in that sense than anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like in Auschwitz and the other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Auschwitz, the people who were not in what they called labor camps-- and there were some labor camps in Auschwitz, to where people who were taken out to-- to work in various installations. There would be only some black fluid. It certainly wasn't coffee. It must have been chicory, sometimes a little more sweet, sometimes less sweet, in the morning. And in the evening, there would be a piece of bread. I think it was 20 grams. And some kind of slop that was called soup, which some time consisted of some potato peels or little pieces of some vegetable. You know, this is-- it's all a very complicated story that-- like everything about the Holocaust. The bread, you know, was obviously spiked, and certainly in the later days, with sawdust. The soups were really slop. And there was, in labor camps, there was supposed to have been at least some residue of meat in it. And you have to know that although Germany was a very orderly and well-organized country, their human systems are very imperfect. And there was plenty of corruption, particularly at that let slay-- late stage in the war for a whole variety of reasons, political and practical. But the most practical reason was that the Germans also were lacking quite a bit of everything. And so the-- the SS were stealing the camps blind from whatever monies that they were supplied with, or what they were collecting as payment. We were hired out to other institutions and structures in the Nazi state. And they collected whatever they collected for us, and then they supposedly administered and operated the camps out of it. And they were stealing, as they were stealing from the food supply that was destined toward the-- for the prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, these-- there is no way in which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anything about the camps or the Holocaust can be adequately described. Let me really say this. I-- I'm always bothered by the notion that the Holocaust is knowable or can be described. The Holocaust is going to be the longest untold story. There is no way in which the real truth of the Holocaust can be described, conveyed, or communicated to the world, to the outside world-- that is, to those who were not there. It is not one story. It is the story of every survivor. And then there is the six million stories that will never be told, that cannot be told. This is quite apart from such prosaic matters, as you know, although surprising-- and we keep being surprised-- at how much written documentation and material can be found about the concentration camp. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Philip, we're going to need to take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry? We need to

speaker: interviewer
text: Take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure.
